title: Kung Pao Chicken
description: This authentic Kung Pao Chicken offers a delightful balance of savory, sweet, sour, and the signature numbing-spicy 'mala' flavor of Sichuan cuisine. Tender velveted chicken, crunchy peanuts, and fragrant dried chilies create a complex and utterly satisfying texture and taste experience, making it a beloved classic for any meal.

ingredients.notes: Divided (1 tbsp for marinade, 1 tbsp for sauce)
Divided (1 tbsp for marinade, 2 tbsp for sauce)
Divided (1 tsp for marinade, 1 tsp for sauce)
Sichuan Tianying or facing heaven, cut into 1-inch pieces, seeds shaken out (adjust to taste)
Divided (2 tbsp for chicken, 1 tbsp for stir-fry)
White and light green parts only, cut into 1/2-inch sections

instructions.title: Marinate the Chicken
instructions.text: Cut the chicken thighs into 3/4-inch cubes. In a medium bowl, combine the chicken with 1 tbsp Shaoxing wine, 1 tbsp light soy sauce, 1 tsp cornstarch, 1 tsp sesame oil, and a pinch of white pepper. Mix thoroughly until the chicken is well coated. Let it marinate at room temperature for at least 15 minutes, or refrigerate for up to 30 minutes. This velveting step is crucial for tender chicken.

instructions.title: Prepare the Kung Pao Sauce
instructions.text: In a small bowl, whisk together the remaining 1 tbsp Shaoxing wine, 2 tbsp light soy sauce, 1 tbsp dark soy sauce, 2 tbsp Chinkiang vinegar, 1.5 tbsp granulated sugar, the remaining 1 tsp cornstarch, and 3 tbsp chicken stock or water. Mix until the sugar is dissolved and the cornstarch is fully incorporated. Set aside.

instructions.title: Prepare Aromatics and Chilies
instructions.text: Gather and prepare all your aromatics: cut dried red chilies into 1-inch pieces (shaking out most of the seeds for less heat), measure out Sichuan peppercorns, mince ginger and garlic, and slice scallions. Ensure everything is ready and within easy reach, as the stir-frying process is very fast.

instructions.title: Cook the Chicken
instructions.text: Heat 2 tbsp of vegetable oil in a well-seasoned wok or large heavy-bottomed skillet over medium-high heat until shimmering. Add the marinated chicken cubes in a single layer (cook in two batches if necessary to avoid overcrowding, which will steam the chicken instead of searing it). Stir-fry for 2-3 minutes until the chicken is just cooked through and lightly browned. Remove the chicken from the wok and set aside.

instructions.title: Infuse Aromatics and Spices
instructions.text: Add another 1 tbsp of vegetable oil to the wok (if needed). Reduce the heat to medium. Add the dried red chilies and Sichuan peppercorns. Stir-fry for about 30 seconds to 1 minute until fragrant and the chilies turn a darker red, being careful not to burn them (which can make them bitter). Immediately add the minced ginger and garlic, stir-frying for another 30 seconds until fragrant.

instructions.title: Combine and Finish
instructions.text: Return the cooked chicken to the wok. Add the sliced scallions and the unsalted roasted peanuts. Give the prepared Kung Pao sauce a quick re-whisk (as cornstarch tends to settle) and pour it evenly over the ingredients in the wok. Increase the heat to high and stir-fry continuously for 1-2 minutes until the sauce thickens and coats all the ingredients, creating a glossy, enticing finish. Serve immediately with steamed rice.

tips: **Mise en Place is Key:** Have all your ingredients prepped and ready before you start cooking, as stir-frying moves very quickly.
**Wok Heat:** Ensure your wok is properly heated before adding oil and ingredients to achieve the characteristic 'wok hei' (breath of the wok) flavor.
**Don't Burn Chilies:** Keep a close eye on the dried chilies and Sichuan peppercorns; they can burn quickly and turn bitter. Adjust heat as necessary.
**Velveting:** The cornstarch in the marinade is essential for tender chicken. Don't skip this step!
**Fresh Peanuts:** For best flavor and crunch, use freshly roasted, unsalted peanuts. If using raw, roast them gently in a dry pan until fragrant before adding.
article:
## History of Kung Pao Chicken

Kung Pao Chicken, known as Gong Bao Ji Ding (宫保鸡丁) in Chinese, is a quintessential dish from the Sichuan province, famed for its bold and complex flavors. The dish is named after Ding Baozhen (1820–1886), a late Qing Dynasty official who served as the governor of Sichuan. His official title was 'Gong Bao,' meaning 'Palace Guardian.' Legend has it that the dish was either invented by him personally or by his household cook, featuring the distinctive numbing-spicy (mala), savory, sweet, and sour profile that defines Sichuan cuisine. It quickly gained popularity and has since become a global icon of Chinese cooking, evolving slightly in different regions but always maintaining its core components of diced chicken, dried chilies, and peanuts.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe for Kung Pao Chicken truly captures the authentic essence of the dish through a meticulous balance of traditional flavors and cooking techniques. The chicken is 'velveted' using a cornstarch and Shaoxing wine marinade, which ensures it remains incredibly tender and moist, even after rapid stir-frying. The sauce, a harmonious blend of light and dark soy sauce, Chinkiang vinegar, sugar, and Shaoxing wine, delivers the complex savory, sweet, and sour notes crucial to the dish's identity. Furthermore, the initial stir-frying of dried chilies and Sichuan peppercorns in oil infuses the entire dish with the signature 'mala' (numbing-spicy) sensation, creating a truly addictive and authentic experience that tantalizes the palate with every bite.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

For the most authentic and delicious Kung Pao Chicken, always opt for boneless, skinless chicken thighs; their higher fat content ensures a juicier and more tender result than breast meat. Achieving 'wok hei' (the 'breath of the wok') is paramount for stir-frying; ensure your wok is smoking hot before adding any oil or ingredients. Prepare all your ingredients (mise en place) before you begin cooking, as the stir-frying process is incredibly fast and requires your full attention. When stir-frying the dried chilies and Sichuan peppercorns, maintain a medium heat to prevent them from burning, which can introduce an undesirable bitter taste. Lastly, always give your prepared sauce a quick whisk right before adding it to the wok, as cornstarch tends to settle at the bottom.

## Serving Suggestions

Kung Pao Chicken is traditionally served immediately, piping hot, over a generous portion of freshly steamed white rice. The rich, glossy sauce perfectly coats the rice, making for a satisfying and complete meal. To create a more elaborate Chinese feast, consider pairing it with complementary dishes such as stir-fried seasonal greens like garlicky bok choy or gai lan, a light and comforting clear egg drop soup, or a simple vegetable stir-fry. It also pairs wonderfully with plain noodles or a side of savory pan-fried dumplings. The bold, complex flavors of Kung Pao Chicken make it a versatile and deeply comforting main course for any lunch or dinner occasion.
